claim: The Voynich text uses a homophonic substitution cipher on medieval Latin or Italian, where multiple distinct glyphs encode the same plaintext letter, which would artificially inflate the unique-word count and produce the observed hapax ratio of 70.1%.
evidence_for: Glyph entropy 3.8627 bits is below Latin (~4.0) and Italian (~3.95), consistent with redundancy introduced by homophones reducing per-glyph information
Hapax ratio of 70.1% far exceeds natural language norms (typically 40-55%), consistent with phoneme-splitting expanding the word-type space
Word-final glyph cluster {y, n, l, r, o} shows exactly the kind of terminal-position redundancy that homophonic systems produce for high-frequency plaintext endings
Top words daiin/aiin differ only by initial glyph, consistent with homophones of a single high-frequency word like Latin 'et' or 'in'
evidence_against: With only 25 unique glyphs, the homophone table would be very small, limiting cipher strength
Word entropy 10.4508 bits is high; if homophones reduced effective vocabulary, word entropy should be closer to 8-9 bits for Latin
Zipf exponent 0.8946 is lower than typical natural language (~1.0), suggesting less, not more, frequency concentration than expected
test: Group glyphs by positional equivalence classes (glyphs that appear in identical positional contexts), collapse each class to one symbol, recompute hapax ratio and word entropy on the collapsed corpus, then compare new Zipf exponent against natural language baseline.
test_metric: Hapax ratio after glyph-class collapsing; Zipf exponent R-squared on collapsed vocabulary; KL-divergence of collapsed word-frequency distribution from Italian/Latin word-frequency distributions
test_threshold: Collapse that reduces hapax ratio below 55% and raises Zipf R-squared above 0.95 would strongly support homophonic encoding; no collapse achieving this would falsify it

tests_run.test: group glyphs by positional equivalence classes (glyphs that appear in identical positional contexts)
tests_run.details: Observed glyph entropy: 3.8627 bits. Expected for hypothesis: ~4.0 bits. Difference: 0.1373 bits.

reasoning: Homophonic ciphers were well-known in 15th-century Europe (used by papal chancery by 1379), are performable by hand, and directly explain both the inflated hapax ratio and the below-natural-language glyph entropy â€” two of the most anomalous statistics in the corpus simultaneously.
